title: English muffins, griddle-baked
subtitle: The split-and-toasted breakfast bread, cooked on a flat pan
excerpt: Twelve English muffins cooked on a dry griddle pan: enriched yeasted dough at 65% hydration, cornmeal-dusted on the outside, cooked low and slow until the sides turn a pale gold. The nooks come from the fork-splitting, not the dough.
sourceNotes: Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861), Project Gutenberg #10136: the muffin entry describes a softened yeasted dough cooked on a greased griddle, split with a fork and toasted — the same structure as the modern English muffin. Hannah Glasse, The Art of Cookery Made Plain and Easy (1747), Project Gutenberg: griddle bread baked without an oven is a recurring pattern throughout. Florence White, Good Things in England (1932): the regional English muffin tradition varies by county (the London muffin was a softer, wetter dough than the Birmingham version) but the griddle method is consistent.

recipe.freezeNotes: Cool fully, freeze in a bag. Toast from frozen — the low moisture content means they toast very fast; watch them.
recipe.batchNotes: Doubles cleanly to 24 — cook in batches on the griddle.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: The dough can proof overnight in the fridge; bring back to room temperature before cutting and cooking.

recipeTools.notes: Useful for kneading, but this dough is manageable by hand.
Or a heavy frying pan. Dry — no fat.

glossaryTerms.definition: Pressing a floured finger lightly into proved dough. If the indent springs back slowly and partially, the dough is ready. If it springs back immediately, it needs more time. If it doesn't spring back at all, it is over-proved.

content.text: English muffins are griddle bread — cooked low and slow on a dry flat pan, not baked in an oven. They start as a standard enriched yeasted dough, cut into rounds, dusted with cornmeal or fine semolina so they don't stick, and cooked on the hob for about 8 minutes per side until the top and base are set and faintly coloured. A brief finish in a warm oven dries the centre. The structure that makes them good for toasting : the rough, nook-filled interior comes from fork-splitting after baking, not from the dough itself.

The griddle temperature matters more than most recipes suggest. Too high and the outside sets before the centre cooks through; too low and the muffin steams rather than baking, and the crust never develops. Medium-low heat, a heavy pan, and patience produce a muffin that is cooked all the way through with a thin, pale-gold crust on both sides.
What you need

items.prepNote: for dusting — or use cornmeal if you have it

content.text: Plain flour gives the muffin the soft, slightly dense crumb that toasts well. Strong bread flour produces a chewier result that pulls apart with more bite.
Method
Mix and knead

Combine the {{plain-flour}}, {{yeast-fast-action}}, {{caster-sugar}}, and {{sea-salt-fine}} in a large bowl, keeping the yeast and salt on opposite sides until mixed. Warm the {{whole-milk}} and {{water}} together to about 35°C (it should feel comfortably warm against your wrist, not hot). Add the warm liquid and the {{unsalted-butter}} to the dry ingredients and mix to a soft, slightly sticky dough. Knead by hand on an unfloured surface for 10 minutes, or use a stand mixer with a dough hook for 7 minutes on medium speed. The dough should be smooth, soft, and only faintly tacky — it should not leave dough on your palm when you pull your hand away.
Bulk ferment, 60 minutes

Cover the bowl with a damp tea towel and leave at room temperature for 60 minutes, until roughly doubled in size. The enrichment from the butter and milk slows the yeast slightly compared to a lean dough; 60 minutes in a warm kitchen should be enough.
Cut and proof

Tip the dough onto a lightly floured surface and roll to a thickness of 2 cm. Cut out rounds with an 8–9 cm cutter dipped in flour, pressing straight down without twisting. Gather the scraps, re-roll once, and cut further rounds until the dough is used up. Scatter the fine semolina over a baking tray and place the cut rounds on it; dust the tops generously with more semolina. The semolina prevents sticking and gives the muffins their characteristic slightly gritty exterior. Cover loosely and leave to proof for 30 minutes. Do the
proof test
: a pressed finger should leave an indent that springs back slowly. If the dough snaps back immediately, give it 10 more minutes.
Griddle-cook, 8 minutes per side

Preheat the oven to 150°C fan. Heat a dry griddle pan or heavy frying pan over medium-low heat (it should feel warm held 5 cm above it, not scorching). Cook the muffins in batches of three or four, leaving space between them. Cook for 8 minutes per side; the muffins should be a pale gold — a deeper colour means the middle has not cooked through. Transfer the cooked batch to a baking tray and keep them in the oven while you cook the rest.
Oven finish

Once all the muffins are griddle-cooked, leave them in the oven at 150°C fan for a further 5–8 minutes. The oven dries the centre and ensures there is no raw dough remaining. A probe through the side of the thickest muffin should read at least 92°C. Cool on a rack for 10 minutes before splitting.
To serve

Split with a fork around the circumference of the muffin rather than cutting with a knife. Fork-splitting tears the interior in a way that creates the rough, uneven surface that toasting later turns into crisp nooks. Cut muffins toast flatter and more uniformly — perfectly fine, but a different result.
Troubleshooting

attrs.heading: When things go wrong
attrs.intro: The two problems that affect griddle-cooked muffins.

items.symptom: Muffins are dark on the outside but raw or doughy in the centre
items.cause: Griddle temperature too high; the crust set before the centre could cook through.
items.fix: Turn the heat to medium-low and be patient. The muffin should reach an even pale-gold colour across 8 minutes — not pale after 4 minutes and dark after 6. If this happens, finish the batch in the oven at 150°C fan for 15 minutes.

items.symptom: Muffins are dense and didn't rise on the griddle
items.cause: Dough under-proofed before cooking, or yeast was old.
items.fix: The proofed rounds should feel noticeably puffy before they go on the griddle. If the indent from the proof test doesn't close at all, they have not proofed enough. Give them 15 more minutes and try again.
